CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (Intro → Related Work → Design Patterns → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (14 design patterns and several novel architectures such as FoF, SBN, TSN). Notation is minimal and consistent, and the narrative explains patterns and trade-offs clearly. Presentation issues: several paragraphs are duplicated near the end and there are a few typos/wording slips (e.g., “duel stream”), and figures/tables are referenced but not shown in the provided text which reduces readability of the results discussion. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized (Intro -> Related Work -> Design Patterns -> Experiments -> Conclusion) and states its main contributions early (14 design patterns and novel architectures such as Fractal-of-FractalNet, SBN, TSN in the Introduction and Section 4). Notation is minimal and consistent (the single Taylor expansion is clear) and the prose explaining choices (e.g., joining methods in Section 3.2.1) is readable. Presentation suffers from some issues: figures/tables are frequently referenced (Table 1, Figures 2/3) but not shown in the provided text, and there are duplicated/paragraphed repetitions and a few grammatical/formatting glitches toward the end that make parts harder to follow.

Presentation issues: several paragraphs are duplicated near the end and there are a few typos/wording slips (e.g., “duel stream”), and figures/tables are referenced but not shown in the provided text which reduces readability of the results discussion.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s main novelty is a synthesis/perspective: a curated set of 14 "design patterns" for CNN architecture (a useful organizational contribution) rather than a fundamentally new problem formulation or theory. It does propose a few new architectural variants (Fractal-of-FractalNet, freeze-drop-path/Stagewise Boosting Networks, and Taylor Series Networks), but these are incremental combinations/extensions of existing ideas (e.g., FractalNet, drop-path/stochastic depth, freezing weights, and polynomial/multiplicative networks) and the empirical gains are modest. The authors cite and largely differentiate their patterns from prior work, but most ‘‘patterns’’ summarize known principles (ResNets, batch-norm, Inception/fractal designs, pyramid/channel scaling), so the contribution is moderate novelty rather than a highly original breakthrough. | B(3): The paper primarily synthesizes existing ideas into a set of 14 "design patterns" (Sections 3 and 3.2.1) rather than introducing a radically new theory; this is a useful perspective but conceptually a re-packaging of known principles (e.g., BN, skip connections, FractalNet, drop-path). The proposed architectural variants (Fractal of FractalNet, freeze-drop-path / Stagewise Boosting Networks, Taylor Series Networks) are novel instantiations but are incremental extensions built from prior work (FractalNet (Larsson et al. 2016), drop-path (Huang et al. 2016b), polynomial/multiplicative ideas (Livni et al. 2014; Lin et al. 2015)), with empirical results showing modest differences. Overall this is a moderate-novelty contribution: a clarifying, organized perspective plus some non-trivial but not groundbreaking architectural tweaks.

It does propose a few new architectural variants (Fractal-of-FractalNet, freeze-drop-path/Stagewise Boosting Networks, and Taylor Series Networks), but these are incremental combinations/extensions of existing ideas (e.g., FractalNet, drop-path/stochastic depth, freezing weights, and polynomial/multiplicative networks) and the empirical gains are modest. The authors cite and largely differentiate their patterns from prior work, but most ‘‘patterns’’ summarize known principles (ResNets, batch-norm, Inception/fractal designs, pyramid/channel scaling), so the contribution is moderate novelty rather than a highly original breakthrough.

The proposed architectural variants (Fractal of FractalNet, freeze-drop-path / Stagewise Boosting Networks, Taylor Series Networks) are novel instantiations but are incremental extensions built from prior work (FractalNet (Larsson et al. 2016), drop-path (Huang et al. 2016b), polynomial/multiplicative ideas (Livni et al. 2014; Lin et al. 2015)), with empirical results showing modest differences. Overall this is a moderate-novelty contribution: a clarifying, organized perspective plus some non-trivial but not groundbreaking architectural tweaks.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated (14 design patterns plus three new architectures) and are partially supported by experiments on CIFAR-10/100 comparing variants to a FractalNet baseline (e.g. FoF trains faster with similar final accuracy, SBN/TSN train faster but have lower final accuracy). Mathematical arguments are informal (e.g. Appendix A’s concatenation vs summation discussion) but not evidently incorrect. However, the experimental methodology is limited: results appear to be single-run comparisons on only CIFAR datasets, no multiple seeds or statistical tests, no reported parameter counts or run-time/compute comparisons, and few baseline architectures or hyperparameter searches; several general design claims are therefore only anecdotal. Given these shortcomings but no fundamental technical errors, the paper is technically acceptable but needs more rigorous empirical validation. | B(3): The paper clearly states a set of 14 design-pattern claims (Section 3) and offers small-scale experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and new architectures (Section 4) that provide some empirical support (e.g., FoF trains faster with similar final accuracy). However, the mathematical arguments (Appendix A) are informal and not formal proofs, and novel ideas like TSN (Taylor squaring) lack theoretical justification. The experimental methodology is limited: comparisons are primarily to a single FractalNet baseline, results are from single runs averaged over iterations (no multiple seeds or statistical tests), and hyperparameter/ablation choices are not exhaustively explored, leaving some claims under-supported. Overall the technical content is plausible and partially validated but has notable gaps in formal justification and experimental rigor.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated (14 design patterns plus three new architectures) and are partially supported by experiments on CIFAR-10/100 comparing variants to a FractalNet baseline (e.g. FoF trains faster with similar final accuracy, SBN/TSN train faster but have lower final accuracy). Mathematical arguments are informal (e.g. Appendix A’s concatenation vs summation discussion) but not evidently incorrect. However, the experimental methodology is limited: results appear to be single-run comparisons on only CIFAR datasets, no multiple seeds or statistical tests, no reported parameter counts or run-time/compute comparisons, and few baseline architectures or hyperparameter searches; several general design claims are therefore only anecdotal. Given these shortcomings but no fundamental technical errors, the paper is technically acceptable but needs more rigorous empirical validation.

However, the mathematical arguments (Appendix A) are informal and not formal proofs, and novel ideas like TSN (Taylor squaring) lack theoretical justification. The experimental methodology is limited: comparisons are primarily to a single FractalNet baseline, results are from single runs averaged over iterations (no multiple seeds or statistical tests), and hyperparameter/ablation choices are not exhaustively explored, leaving some claims under-supported. Overall the technical content is plausible and partially validated but has notable gaps in formal justification and experimental rigor.
